front: Concept: preferred endonym of the Berbers; "Berber" itself is an exonym now often considered pejorative
Language: North African language of the Berbers, historically written in the Tifinagh script
Concept: a "Neo" alphabetic version of this ancient Berber script was adopted by Morocco's king in 2003
Ruler: Moroccan king who elevated the Tamazight language to official status in 2011
Language: Berber dialect notable for allowing any consonant to serve as a syllable nucleus
Place: mountain ranges of Morocco where Berbers are concentrated today
Concept: category of North African pirates whose name derives from the Berbers
Figure: English pirate who introduced square-rigged ships to the Berbers
People: indigenous people of the Canary Islands considered the closest living relatives of the Berbers
Product: prized Moroccan oil long harvested by Berber women's cooperatives in the Sous region, later popularized in 21st-century cosmetics
Place: Libyan region, home to the city of Zuwara and the Nufasa mountains, considered a historical Berber homeland
Concept: anarchic, ungoverned regions of Morocco historically inhabited primarily by Berbers
Ruler: united the Massylii subgroup of the Berbers into the Kingdom of Numidia
Ruler: captured his rival Syphax after aiding Rome at the Battle of the Great Plains
Place: capital of a Roman client kingdom ruled by Juba II, populated by Berbers
Place: Roman province, divided into the "Tingitana" and "Caesariensis" regions, primarily inhabited by Berbers
Ruler: Numidian king paraded by Gaius Marius in a triumph after the namesake Jugurthine War
Concept: violent bands formed by heretical Christian Berber martyrs in Roman North Africa
Author: Berber writer who presented an "Apology" at Sabratha against accusations of using witchcraft to woo a wealthy woman
Ruler: last ruler of a Berber kingdom that split into eight successor kingdoms, including Altava, after his defeat
Figure: Byzantine governor who opposed a Berber king allied with the rebel Stotzas
Document: inscription recording Egypt's defeat of the Berber-ancestor chieftain Meryey's alliance with the Lukka and Sea Peoples
work: The Meshwesh and the Libyan Dynasties of Egypt

front: Document: one of the first documents to mention "Israel," commemorating an Egyptian victory over Berber ancestors
work: The Meshwesh and the Libyan Dynasties of Egypt

front: Concept: name Egyptians gave to the Berber-descended rulers of Egypt's 22nd and 23rd Dynasties
work: The Meshwesh and the Libyan Dynasties of Egypt

front: Ruler: pharaoh, identified with the biblical Shishak, whose accession begins a Berber calendar era divided into four "Gates of the Year"
work: The Meshwesh and the Libyan Dynasties of Egypt

front: People: ancient desert civilization of Libya's Fezzan region, famed for building extensive aqueducts
Concept: distinctive Garamantian pottery decoration technique providing the northernmost evidence of it in Africa
Figure: Berber rebel whose revolt against Rome was supported by the Garamantes
Figure: Roman general who attacked the Garamantes in the Fezzan in 19 BCE during the first Roman trans-Saharan expedition
Ruler: Roman emperor who captured the Garamantian capital, Garama, in 202 CE
Ruler: Christian Berber king who killed the Umayyad general Uqba ibn Nafi at the Battle of Vescera (682)
work: Kusaila and Kahina: Resistance to the Umayyad Conquest

front: Ruler: Berber king killed near Mems while resisting an invasion led by Zuhayr ibn Qays
work: Kusaila and Kahina: Resistance to the Umayyad Conquest

front: Ruler: succeeded Kusaila as leader of Berber resistance and won the decisive Battle of Meskiana (698) against Hassan ibn al-Nu'man
work: Kusaila and Kahina: Resistance to the Umayyad Conquest

front: Ruler: member of the Zenata confederation whose faith -- Jewish, Christian, or pagan -- is disputed; one account says she carried a wooden idol suggesting conversion from Judaism to Christianity
work: Kusaila and Kahina: Resistance to the Umayyad Conquest

front: Ruler: ruled from the Aures region and, per legend, could see into the future, supposedly living to be 127 years old
work: Kusaila and Kahina: Resistance to the Umayyad Conquest

front: Ruler: practiced scorched-earth tactics against her own people's cities and fortified the Roman amphitheater at El Djem, Tunisia
work: Kusaila and Kahina: Resistance to the Umayyad Conquest

front: Ruler: was ultimately defeated by the Umayyad general Musa bin Nusayr, ending organized Berber resistance to the Arab conquest
work: Kusaila and Kahina: Resistance to the Umayyad Conquest

front: Figure: Berber general who led the 711 Umayyad conquest of al-Andalus, giving his name to the Strait of Gibraltar
work: Tariq ibn Ziyad and the Conquest of Spain

front: Figure: defeated the Visigothic King Roderic at the decisive Battle of Guadalete
work: Tariq ibn Ziyad and the Conquest of Spain

front: Place: Visigothic capital captured by Tariq ibn Ziyad after the Battle of Guadalete, later displaced by Seville and then Cordoba as center of al-Andalus
work: Tariq ibn Ziyad and the Conquest of Spain

front: Event: 739 Berber revolt, originally led by Maysara, that failed to capture the city of Kairouan
Event: Berber fighters tied bags of water to the tails of mares to trigger a stampede that neutralized an enemy cavalry force sent by Caliph Hisham
Event: the 740s Great Berber Revolt was partly inspired by preachers of this Islamic sect
Event: this Berber revolt, whose fighters made up the majority of the Umayyad army in Iberia, forced an Umayyad withdrawal from northern Spain that allowed the Kingdom of Asturias to survive
Empire: Berber dynasty, encouraged by religious leaders of the Sanhaja confederation, whose name derives from desert frontier fortresses called "ribat"
Empire: Berber dynasty nicknamed "the muffled ones" for the veils its members wore over their mouths
Place: oasis city controlled by the Almoravids, the northern terminus of the trans-Saharan trade route to the salt mines of Taghaza
Battle: 1086 Almoravid victory won after being invited by the taifa princes of al-Andalus to defend against King Alfonso VI of Castile
Figure: founded the Almohad movement by uniting the Masmuda tribe (distinct from the Zanata and Sanhaja), overthrowing the ruling Almoravids

Empire: Berber caliphate that lost to Alfonso VIII of Castile at Las Navas de Tolosa (1212) and was destroyed when its last leader, Idris II, was assassinated and the Marinids seized its capital, Marrakech
People: nomadic, Tamasheq-speaking Saharan people traditionally called the "Blue People" for their indigo-dyed "tagelmust" veils
Institution: Tuareg confederations, such as one named for the Hoggar Mountains, led by chiefs called "amenokal"
Figure: 4th-century CE queen, ancestress of the Tuareg, whose tomb at Abalessa was controversially excavated in 1925 by Count Byron de Prorok
Figure: recently-canonized Catholic missionary who lived among the Tuareg
Figure: led an Ottoman-backed Tuareg rebellion during World War I
Event: 2012 rebellion by the secular MNLA and Islamist Ansar Dine seeking Tuareg independence for the northern Malian region of Azawad
Figure: founder of the Islamist group Ansar Dine, active in the 2012 Tuareg rebellion
Event: 2007 Tuareg uprising in Niger protesting uranium mining at Arlit
Band: Tuareg musical group that emerged from conflict with the Toubou people
Event: in 2023, Tuareg rebels blamed this Russian private military group, whose leader died two months after his own mutiny against Moscow, for an attack that killed two men
Film: Abderrahmane Sissako's most famous film, fictionalizing the execution of a Tuareg cattle herder in its title city
Event: the Tuareg opposed this Libyan leader's government during the Libyan Civil War
Concept: French colonial ideology portraying the Kabyle subgroup as more assimilable, "hardworking, enterprising, practical" than Arabs
Figure: Berber woman who joined Algeria's War of Independence; historian Natalya Vince argues the Kabyle myth made French authorities skeptical women like her acted independently
Concept: term for Berber soldiers recruited into the French army after the conquest of Algeria, known for baggy red trousers
Figure: Berber leader who led the Rif War (Second Moroccan War) against Spain, whose Disaster of Annual defeated General Manuel Fernandez Silvestre in 1921
Ruler: Moroccan king, and the country's first post-independence leader, who invited Abd el Krim back from exile
Event: 1980 namesake protests that broke out in Algeria's Kabylie region
Work: Pierre Bourdieu's ethnographic study analyzing the "homologous oppositions" (e.g. man/woman) structuring Kabyle Berber homes
back: The Kabyle House, or the World Reversed (Bourdieu)

front: Work: Ernest Gellner book discussing the authority of Berber hereditary "marabout" saints to argue for segmentary lineage theory
Work: Clifford Geertz book comparing the Berber saint Al-Yusi to the Javanese saint Kalijaga to analyze "the antipodes of the Muslim world"
Work: Ibn Khaldun history, whose full title also names the Arabs, that was the first to distinguish the Berber tribes
Author: historian who created an encyclopedia devoted to the Berbers
Figure: 17th-century historian who recorded a legend of a Berber polymath who attempted to fly by attaching feathers to himself
